text: Hey everyone, I'm Shaw. In this video, I'm going to give a beginnerfriendly guide to GitHub. I'll start by describing what GitHub is and some key concepts and then walk through how to set up your GitHub environment and start working on your first repo. What is GitHub? This is just a platform for sharing and managing software projects. If you spent any time in AI engineering or software development, you've probably come across GitHub before. But you might be wondering why should I use it? So there are many benefits to using GitHub. First and foremost, it's a free and simple way to share your work with the world. The second is all the collaboration tools and features that it has. So pull requests, creating issues, code review, guies, conbon boards, basically anything you need to productively manage a software project is available in GitHub. And this is probably one of the main reasons why GitHub is so popular is because it makes it very easy for a large number of contributors to collaborate productively on a single codebase. It also has GitHub actions so you can create these continuous integration or continuous deployment pipelines and automate different workflows that you might have for your project. Has wikis and documentation built into it. gives you a really nice guey for browsing repos, commits, branches, and diffs. And so you can do all of this stuff in their web UI. A common confusion point for people getting into GitHub is the difference between GitHub and Git. While these two tools go handinand, they are not the same thing. Git is a tool that tracks changes to your code, while GitHub is a platform where you can share and collaborate on Git repositories. So, a helpful analogy here is to think of Git as kind of like the engine that allows GitHub to run. A car is a lot more than just its engine. It's got its body. It's got a steering wheel. It's got the instrument cluster. There are a lot of things that go into a car, but it's the engine that ultimately is what allows it to move forward. Git is essential to GitHub because keeping track of different versions of a codebase is the foundation for all the collaboration tools that GitHub has. So, let's talk about the basics of Git. Because most times when interacting with GitHub, you're going to do it via Git. While there's a laundry list of different terms we can talk about when it comes to Git, here, I'm just going to cover the essentials that you need to know to get started. First and foremost, Git operates in a repository. This is just a folder where file changes are tracked via git. So this versioning tool that we're using, for example, you can have a Python file and you can make some changes to it and then you can track those changes with Git. But this isn't just for code. You can do this for any kind of file. So like a CSV file, a text file, a readme file, like whatever it is. You can use Git to record changes to all the files in your folder. The next term to know is cloning a repo. So in the context of GitHub, what this typically means is you'll have a remote repository. So you'll have a folder with code in it sitting on some GitHub server somewhere. Cloning a repo just means you're going to download that repository on your local machine. Let's say you download a repo and you make some code changes to it. The add function in Git will add your changes to a staging area called the index. This doesn't record the changes yet, but just adds it to this index. To actually record the code changes, you use the commit command. So this is called committing code. This is actually keeping track of different versions of your codebase. So an important note here is that git does not automatically version your code for you. You need to manually do these commits to track changes. So let's say we're working on this repository locally and we've committed our changes. If we want to update a remote repository like one sitting on GitHub, we can use the push command. So that'll update the remote repository based on local changes. And if we want to do the inverse, we can use the pull command. So I'm sure this all sounds very abstract now, but we're going to walk through a concrete workflow of using all these commands in a bit. I'll just talk about one more concept and then we'll go into the hands-on examples. The last thing I want to talk about is this concept of a branch. Branches are essentially splits in a git repo's commit history. What this typically looks like is you'll create a repository and you might have your initial commit. And then if you make some code changes and commit that, then you're saving that version of the codebase. And then you can do that again and again and so on and so forth. So a repos commit history or commit logs is just this linear sequence of versions of that codebase. If you only have one branch of your git repository, it's going to be called the main branch. This is kind of like the master working version of your codebase. While this works fine for most solo projects that are not very complicated, this can easily become a headache if you're working on a pretty big project and you're working with a pretty big team. So, one thing that's helpful is instead of just operating on a single main branch, you can create multiple branches for different tasks, whether it's fixing a specific bug or adding a new feature. So, let's say we want to add a new feature after commit 2. So we can create a new branch called feature. Make the changes to add the feature locally. Then I can push the changes to GitHub. I can submit a pull request so someone can review my code and make sure that everything is good and it's not going to cause any problems. And if there are problems, those conflicts can be resolved all using GitHub's web UI. And then finally, the changes can be merged into the main branch. So, if you're first starting out and you're just working by yourself, you probably won't be dealing with branches. You can just operate by yourself on the main branch. But I think it's important to at least get a basic understanding of how branches work as you use Git and GitHub more and more. Another thing I want to call out here is a pull request. So, most of the stuff we see on the screen here, these are basic Git commands. So, creating branches is a feature of Git. Committing changes is also a feature of Git. Pushing changes is a feature of Git. Resolving conflicts and merging code is a feature of Git. But one thing that Git does not have is this pull request. A pull request is just submitting code changes. Submitting your work for review by some repo admin or community. So this is enabled by GitHub. You can do it in the web UI. And so when you make the pull request, it shows the changes. People can have a discussion about it. And if the changes look good and there are no conflicts, it can be merged into the main branch. The purpose of git ignore is that it's a secret text file that basically tells git which files and folders to ignore. So it won't track changes to those files and folders and it won't push them to GitHub. And so this is really important. If you want to store API keys in AENV file or if you have big data files that you don't want to push to GitHub, you can add those to the git ignore. So there are going to be three commands that we're going to run in succession. And 90% of the time when using git, these are the three commands I'm typing. So it's going to be get add dot. And so get add dot just adds everything in the repo to the staging area. So now we've added everything, but we still need to commit it to the commit log. So to do that we'll do get commit. So this will actually track the changes that we've made. Then we can add a comment to the commit. So I'll just say adding Python file. We do that. But still the remote repository, the one on GitHub hasn't changed. So if we want to make changes to that one, we need to do get push and then this will push the changes that we made locally to GitHub. So now if we go back to GitHub, this is what it looked like originally. And then if we refresh the page, we can see that this main.py file is here now. So that's a basic workflow of using GitHub. You can create a new repo on the web UI. You can clone that repo locally. You can make whatever changes you want and then you can push those changes back to GitHub. Okay, so we covered a ton of material in this video. So I just wanted to conclude with a few key takeaways. First, Git is a tool that tracks changes to your code, which is different from GitHub, which is a platform where you can share and collaborate on Git repositories. Git keeps track of different versions of your codebase via commits, which record snapshots of your code as it changes. Git also has branches, which let you work on new features or fixes without breaking the main code base. pull requests and merges bring those changes back into the main project. And then finally, with GitHub's command line interface, you can work on Git repositories locally and interact with GitHub all from your command line. Okay, so that brings us to the end.
